name: Temporal-Difference Reward-Learning Agent
description: Reinforcement learning agent using temporal-difference (TD) Q-learning with separate liking and wanting channels. The reward signal upon eating combines an opioid-mediated liking component (pure palatability) and a homeostatic relief component, both gated by D2 receptor availability which models reward tolerance from chronic overconsumption. Dopaminergic reward prediction errors drive Q-value updates (wanting/motivation learning). D2 receptors downregulate with repeated palatable food intake and slowly recover, capturing the addiction-like tolerance dynamics described by Volkow et al. (2011). State is discretized as relative position to nearest food and binned hunger level. This formulation produces an agent that learns which actions lead to rewarding food, but whose effective reward sensitivity degrades with chronic overconsumption.

variables.name: hunger_level
variables.description: Discretized energy deficit; increases each step without eating, resets upon eating. Used as part of state representation and to compute homeostatic reward.

variables.name: d2_receptor_availability
variables.description: Reward sensitivity scalar modeling striatal D2 receptor density; decreases with chronic palatable food consumption (tolerance), slowly recovers toward 1.0

variables.name: action_value_table
variables.description: Q-table mapping (state, action) pairs to expected cumulative reward. State is (dx_bin, dy_bin, hunger_bin). Initialized to zeros.

variables.name: liking_reward
variables.description: Opioid-mediated hedonic pleasure upon eating; equals palatability of consumed food (Berridge & Robinson, 1998)

variables.name: homeostatic_reward
variables.description: Reward from reducing energy deficit; proportional to hunger level at time of eating

variables.name: reward_prediction_error
variables.description: Dopaminergic TD error signal; positive = reward better than expected (dopamine burst, increases wanting), negative = worse than expected (dopamine dip)

variables.name: current_state
variables.description: Discretized state tuple: (dx_bin, dy_bin, hunger_bin) where dx_bin/dy_bin are binned relative position to nearest food

parameters.name: learning_rate
parameters.source: Standard TD learning rate; moderate for non-stationary food environment

parameters.name: temporal_discount_factor
parameters.source: Agents prefer sooner rewards; consistent with impulsivity in hedonic eating (Volkow et al., 2011)

parameters.name: d2_downregulation_rate
parameters.source: Slow tolerance mirroring chronic D2 receptor loss in obesity (Volkow et al., 2011)

parameters.name: d2_recovery_rate
parameters.source: Slower recovery than downregulation, asymmetric as in addiction literature (Volkow et al., 2011)

parameters.name: liking_reward_weight
parameters.source: Hedonic component dominates reward signal (Berridge & Robinson, 1998)

parameters.name: homeostatic_reward_weight
parameters.source: Homeostatic relief is rewarding but secondary in palatable contexts

parameters.name: hunger_growth_rate
parameters.source: Calibrated to ~20 steps to reach strong hunger

parameters.name: hunger_aversion_cost
parameters.source: Small negative signal proportional to hunger; aversive cost of unmet homeostatic need

parameters.name: number_of_hunger_bins
parameters.source: Discretization granularity for hunger in state space

parameters.name: number_of_position_bins
parameters.source: Discretization bins for relative position to nearest food: [-far, -near, 0, +near, +far]

rules.description: Hunger accumulation: hunger increases linearly each step without eating, capped at 1.0
rules.pseudocode:
if not ate_last_step: H = min(1.0, H + lambda_H)
if ate_last_step: H = H_reset

rules.description: Composite reward upon eating: liking (palatability) + homeostatic relief, gated by D2 receptor availability
rules.pseudocode:
r_like = consumed_food_palatability
r_homeo = H  # hunger level at time of eating
r = D2 * (w_like * r_like + w_homeo * r_homeo)
rules.source_postulate: P2 (liking and wanting dissociation; Berridge & Robinson, 1998), P5 (D2 gating; Volkow et al., 2011)

rules.description: Reward when not eating: small aversive cost proportional to hunger
TD reward prediction error (dopamine signal): standard temporal-difference error driving wanting updates
rules.pseudocode: delta = r + gamma_TD * max(Q[s_next, a'] for all a') - Q[s_t, a_t]
rules.source_postulate: P6 (dopamine mediates wanting via prediction errors; Berridge & Robinson, 1998; Schultz framework)

rules.description: Q-value update: action-value updated by learning rate times prediction error
rules.pseudocode: Q[s_t, a_t] = Q[s_t, a_t] + alpha_Q * delta

rules.description: D2 receptor downregulation: eating palatable food reduces D2, with slow recovery toward baseline
rules.pseudocode:
if ate_last_step: D2 = D2 - kappa * consumed_food_palatability
D2 = D2 + kappa_r * (1.0 - D2)
D2 = clip(D2, 0.2, 1.0)
rules.source_postulate: P5 (chronic overconsumption downregulates D2 receptors; Volkow et al., 2011)

rules.description: State discretization: agent state is binned relative position to nearest food and binned hunger level
rules.pseudocode:
dx = nearest_food.x - perception.x
dy = nearest_food.y - perception.y
dx_bin = clip(round(dx * n_pos_bins / grid_width), -n_pos_bins, n_pos_bins)
dy_bin = clip(round(dy * n_pos_bins / grid_height), -n_pos_bins, n_pos_bins)
hunger_bin = int(H * n_hunger_bins)
if no food visible: dx_bin = 0; dy_bin = 0
s_t = (dx_bin, dy_bin, hunger_bin)
rules.source_postulate: Discretization for tractable Q-learning in grid world

decision_logic.description: Epsilon-greedy action selection over learned Q-values. The agent learns from composite hedonic+homeostatic reward signals gated by D2 receptor availability. EAT is only considered feasible when on a food tile. Over time, D2 downregulation blunts reward signal, requiring the agent to consume more to achieve the same Q-value updates, modeling tolerance/addiction dynamics.
pseudocode: # 1. Determine previous reward and update internal state
# Record palatability of consumed food if ate
consumed_palatability = stored from last step's food palatability if ate_last_step else 0.0
if ate_last_step: D2 = D2 - kappa * consumed_palatability
# 2. Compute reward signal for last step
nearest = food with min Manhattan distance
max_q_next = max(Q.get((s_new, a), 0.0) for a in all_actions)
delta = r + gamma_TD * max_q_next - Q.get((prev_state, prev_action), 0.0)
Q[(prev_state, prev_action)] = Q.get((prev_state, prev_action), 0.0) + alpha_Q * delta
on_food = any(f.x == perception.x and f.y == perception.y for f in foods)
action = argmax over feasible_actions of Q.get((s_new, a), 0.0)

perception_to_variables.food_sources: perception.resources.food  # list of {x, y, type, palatability}
perception_to_variables.consumed_food_palatability: stored from food at agent position before eat action; retrieved from perception.resources.food where f.x == perception.x and f.y == perception.y
perception_to_variables.ate_last_step: determined in update() method: action was 'eat' and reward > 0

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action → ConsumeEffect reward of 1.0; this env reward signals successful consumption, but the agent internally computes its own composite reward (r_like + r_homeo gated by D2)

expected_behaviors.description: Agent learns to approach and eat food: Q-values for actions moving toward food increase over episodes
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 500 steps → compare Q-values for move_toward_food vs move_away_from_food actions → assert toward > away

expected_behaviors.description: D2 receptor downregulation with chronic overconsumption (P5): after many eating events, D2 decreases
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 200 steps with abundant high-palatability food → assert D2 < 0.8

expected_behaviors.description: Blunted reward from tolerance: effective reward per meal decreases as D2 declines
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: compare r for first eat (D2~1.0) vs 50th eat (D2~0.6) with same palatability → r_50th < r_1st

expected_behaviors.description: Positive prediction error for unexpected palatable food (dopamine burst)
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: agent encounters food with high palatability for first time → delta > 0 (reward better than expected from Q=0)

expected_behaviors.description: Hedonic reward dominates: agent prefers high-palatability food over low-palatability food even at low hunger
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: after learning, place two foods equidistant (palatability 1.0 vs 0.1) → agent approaches palatability 1.0

expected_behaviors.description: Hunger aversion: agent experiences small negative reward accumulation when hungry and not eating
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 20 steps without food → cumulative r is negative and proportional to hunger growth

references: Berridge & Robinson (1998) — What is the role of dopamine in reward: hedonic impact, reward learning, and incentive salience? — Brain Research Reviews (PubMed 9858756)
Volkow et al. (2011) — Hedonic mechanisms and reward hyperfunction in obesity — as cited in PMC5940344
Lutter & Nestler (2009) — Homeostatic and Hedonic Signals Interact in the Regulation of Food Intake — Journal of Nutrition (PMC2714382)
AJPH (2014) — Dynamic Interplay Among Homeostatic, Hedonic, and Cognitive Feedback Circuits Regulating Body Weight — doi:10.2105/AJPH.2014.301931
Berridge (2004) — Incentive theory of motivation applied to ingestive behavior — as cited in ScienceDirect S0195666322002379
Toates (1986) — Incentive theory of motivation — as cited in ScienceDirect S0195666322002379
Kelley & Berridge (2002) — The Need to Feed: Homeostatic and Hedonic Control of Eating — Neuron (ScienceDirect S0896627302009698)
